summary: Crown-jewel property is integrity: only the right people approve, and the audit trail cannot be forged. Biggest gap in the Brief: it conflates SSO authentication with authorization (a1) — SSO proves identity, it does not decide who may approve, and nothing stops an engineer approving their own deploy. Mandatory: an approver allowlist + separation of duties (requester ≠ approver) + DB-enforced append-only audit. Not regulated — no external compliance regime.

threats.threat: an engineer approves their own deploy request, defeating the control
threats.mitigation: enforce requester ≠ approver server-side, plus an explicit approver-role check

threats.threat: audit records are altered or deleted to hide who authorized a release
threats.mitigation: append-only audit_event with UPDATE/DELETE revoked at the DB grant; no app path to mutate

threats.threat: any authenticated SSO user can call the approve endpoint
threats.mitigation: authorization check on the lead role, not merely a valid session

authn: GitHub OAuth via the existing SSO gateway (brief.constraints.c3). Identity only — never treated as authorization.
authz: Role-based: an authenticated user may request; only a user in the approver allowlist for the service may approve/reject; a request's own requester may never approve it (separation of duties).
secrets: The deploy-pipeline API token lives in the platform secret store / env, never in the DB or the repo; rotated on the platform's schedule.

data_protection.at_rest: Postgres on the internal platform's encrypted volume
data_protection.in_transit: TLS terminated at the SSO gateway

security_risks.risk: approver allowlist drifts out of date as people change teams
security_risks.mitigation: source the allowlist from the GitHub team membership rather than a hand-maintained list

challenges_to_brief.concern: the Brief assumes GitHub SSO is sufficient for access control, but SSO authenticates identity and does not authorize approval — as written, any engineer could approve any deploy, including their own, which defeats g1
challenges_to_brief.recommendation: add an explicit approver allowlist (sourced from GitHub team membership) and enforce requester ≠ approver server-side
